proposed_short: The congiuntivo presente: che io parli, che lui prenda, che loro siano. The subjunctive form used for present or future reference after a present-tense trigger.
proposed_long: The present subjunctive (congiuntivo presente) is the subjunctive's basic form, used in a subordinate clause introduced by a present-tense trigger of opinion, will, doubt or emotion (penso che, voglio che, dubito che, è importante che). Its endings: -are verbs take -i (parli, parli, parli, parliamo, parliate, parlino); -ere and -ire verbs take -a (prenda, dorma ... prendano, dormano), with the -isc- group inserting -isc- in the singular and 3rd-person plural (capisca, capiscano). The three singular persons are identical (che io parli, che tu parli, che lui parli), so a subject pronoun or the context supplies the person. The large irregular group is built from the 1st-person singular present indicative (vengo gives venga, faccio gives faccia), and a small set of suppletive verbs (sia, abbia, sappia, dia, stia) must be learned outright. Distinct from the imperfect subjunctive (che io parlassi), which a past-tense trigger selects.
rationale: Used throughout the congiuntivo formation grammar and translation explanations. The glossary has a generic 'subjunctive' entry but no entry for the present subjunctive specifically, which is wave 1's whole subject and the form learners meet first. Distinct from 'subjunctive' (the mood in general) and from the imperfect/compound subjunctives authored in later waves.

proposed_short: A form built from a wholly different root than the rest of the verb, so it cannot be derived by rule and must be learned: essere gives sia, avere gives abbia.
proposed_long: A suppletive form is one whose stem comes from a different historical root than the verb's other forms, so no regular rule produces it. In the Italian present subjunctive the clearest cases are essere (sia, siamo, siate, siano) and avere (abbia ... abbiano), plus sapere (sappia), dare (dia) and stare (stia): none of these can be reached from the infinitive or, unlike the larger irregular group, from the 1st-person singular present indicative (sono does not give sia, ho does not give abbia). They have to be memorised. sia and abbia matter most because they are also the auxiliaries of the compound past and pluperfect subjunctives. Contrast with the from-the-1sg irregulars (venga from vengo), which are irregular but still rule-derivable.
rationale: Used in the irregular_suppletive grammar and translation explanations to name why sia/abbia/sappia/dia/stia must be learned rather than derived. The glossary has 'stem expansion' and 'stem change' for rule-governed irregularity but no term for suppletion, which is the distinct concept the suppletive-five leaf rests on.
